title: Animating taichi diagrams
text: taichi (yin-yang) symbol cyclical, motion unusually -brand geom. Instead forcing third variable (e.g. week) onto x-axis shrinking every glyph, can turn animation frame. frame clean category x state grid large, readable taichi. vignette shows combine geom_taichi() gganimate. gganimate Suggested dependency; install install.packages("gganimate") already.
code: library(ggplot2) library(ggtaichi) library(gganimate)

title: Animating taichi diagrams
text: geom_taichi() returns pair layers separated ggnewscale::new_scale_fill(), fish keeps fill scale legend. gganimate works layer level — splits every layer’s data transition variable builds one frame per state. two fish layers ordinary ggplot2 layers, gganimate treats independently two fill scales continue apply frame--frame. short: ggnewscale layer stack composes cleanly gganimate, special wrapper needed. (just theory: every recipe rendered frame--frame gganimate 1.0.11, legends per-fish fills intact every frame.)

title: Animating taichi diagrams
text: Use transition_states() frame variable discrete (e.g. week number), transition_time() continuous time. drive underlying fish layers. bundled states_tg data perfect showcase: instead squeezing 30 weeks onto x-axis, keep category x state grid large glyphs let weeks play frames. fills continuous, tweenr interpolates values smoothly consecutive weeks.
code: p <- ggplot(states_tg, aes(x = category, y = state)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   labs(title = "Week {closest_state}") +   transition_states(week, transition_length = 1, state_length = 1)  # Render to a GIF (requires the gifski package) # animate(p, renderer = gifski_renderer()) # anim_save("taichi.gif", p)

title: Animating taichi diagrams
text: Taichi symbols always drawn round (sized square units, like grid::circleGrob()), coord_fixed() keeps cells square , glyphs fill evenly. Match animation dimensions grid:
code: p_fixed <- ggplot(states_tg, aes(x = category, y = state)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi() +   transition_states(week, transition_length = 1, state_length = 1)  # animate(p_fixed, width = 800, height = 600, fps = 10)

title: Animating taichi diagrams
text: angle argument (see ?geom_taichi) can rotate glyph. Mapping angle expression frame variable animating produces iconic “turning taichi” — eyes = TRUE eye rides around fish’s head: Note state_length = 1 transition_length = 0: frame state, rotation advances crisp steps (least one two lengths must positive, gganimate allocate frames). grow-reveal instead, keep positive transition_length add enter_grow() plot.
code: spin <- data.frame(   x = 1, y = 1, yin = 5, yang = 5,   frame = 1:36 )  p_spin <- ggplot(spin, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin, yang = yang, angle = frame * 10,               eyes = TRUE) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi() +   transition_states(frame, transition_length = 0, state_length = 1)  # animate(p_spin, width = 300, height = 300, fps = 12)

title: Animating taichi diagrams
text: gganimate::anim_save() writes animation file. MP4 output use ffmpeg_renderer() (requires system ffmpeg); GIF use gifski_renderer() (requires gifski package). Aim 10–15 fps enough frames motion smooth; coord_fixed() keeps every glyph round rendered video.

title: Gallery
text: two sources share units, shared_legend = TRUE paints fish one palette one scale, two halves every glyph can read single legend. bundled cafes_tg data (synthetic espresso vs. matcha orders) made :
code: ggplot(cafes_tg, aes(x = week, y = neighbourhood)) +   geom_taichi(yin = matcha, yang = espresso,               shared_legend = TRUE,               yin_name = "orders / 100 customers") +   remove_padding() +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("Espresso (yang) vs matcha (yin), one shared scale")

title: Gallery
text: Keep source’s palette align limits, equal values carry equal ink:
code: ggplot(cafes_tg, aes(x = week, y = neighbourhood)) +   geom_taichi(yin = matcha,   yin_name = "Matcha",               yin_colors  = c("#deebf7", "#3182bd", "#08306b"),               yang = espresso, yang_name = "Espresso",               yang_colors = c("#fee6ce", "#e6550d", "#7f2704"),               shared_limits = TRUE) +   remove_padding() +   theme_taichi()

title: Gallery
code: d <- data.frame(x = 1:4, y = 1, yin = c(2, 4, 6, 8), yang = c(8, 6, 4, 2),                 pull = c(30, 5, 18, 45))  ggplot(d, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin, yang = yang, eyes = TRUE,               yin_eye_size = pull, yang_eye_size = 0.12,               limits = c(0, 10)) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("Eye size as a fifth channel")

title: Gallery
text: Rotation can pure annotation data channel — glyph’s angle encodes column:
code: grid16 <- expand.grid(x = 1:4, y = 1:4) grid16$yin <- seq(1, 10, length.out = 16) grid16$yang <- rev(grid16$yin) grid16$turn <- grid16$x * 22.5  ggplot(grid16, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin, yang = yang, angle = turn, eyes = TRUE,               limits = c(0, 10)) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Gallery
code: d9 <- expand.grid(x = 1:3, y = 1:3) d9$roast  <- factor(c("light", "medium", "dark")[(d9$x + d9$y) %% 3 + 1]) d9$origin <- factor(c("blend", "single")[(d9$x * d9$y) %% 2 + 1])  ggplot(d9, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = roast, yang = origin) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Gallery
text: Dense grids stop symbols read one one become texture two interleaved fields — still useful spotting bands regime changes:
code: ggplot(subset(states_tg, state %in% c("New York", "Texas")),        aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   facet_wrap(~ state, ncol = 1) +   remove_padding() +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("30 weeks as texture")

title: Gallery
text: yin_scale / yang_scale accept fill scale, exported geom_yin_fish() / geom_yang_fish() let assemble everything hand (scales, ggnewscale stacking):
code: ggplot(d, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin, yang = yang,               yin_scale = scale_fill_viridis_c,               yang_scale = scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "yang", option = "magma")) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: heat map drawn ggplot2::geom_tile() carries three dimensions information: x position, y position, single value mapped fill. plenty one number per cell, forces facet (draw two separate maps) moment want compare two data sources footing. ggtaichi removes limitation replacing cell taichi (yin-yang) diagram. symbol circle split S-curve two interlocking “fish”: yang (light) fish shaded one data source, yin (dark) fish shaded . fish live cell, single geom_taichi() layer encodes four dimensions : x, y, yin, yang. two sources keep color scales legends, can read independently still compared side side. default decorative eyes markers – every drop ink plot mapped data – switch classic eyes (eyes = TRUE, new v0.2.0), can carry data , taking single glyph six dimensions.
code: library(ggtaichi) library(ggplot2)

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: worth zooming one cell see anatomy glyph. yang fish (bulb bottom) carries one source; yin fish (bulb top) carries . half filled gradient, lighter darker shade smaller larger value.  yang (red) fish reads 7 yin (grey) fish reads 3; deeper ink, larger number relative rest data.
code: one <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 1, google = 7, twitter = 3)  ggplot(one, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = twitter, yang = google) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: ggtaichi ships data sets used foundational package ggDoubleHeat. pitts_tg records 30-week COVID-related Google Twitter incidence rates 9 categories Pittsburgh Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA). states_tg larger sibling, repeating measurements across four states, pitts_emojis holds popular weekly emoji per category. Since v0.3.0 package also bundles cafes_tg, small synthetic espresso-vs-matcha dataset whose two columns share units — handy shared-scale features shown later. See ?pitts_tg, ?states_tg, ?pitts_emojis, ?cafes_tg full descriptions.
code: head(pitts_tg) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   msa         week week_start category          Twitter Google #>   <chr>      <int> <date>     <chr>               <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Covid              0.965  0.681  #> 2 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 General Virus      0.538  0.0982 #> 3 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Masks              0.466  0.117  #> 4 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Sanitizing         0.0561 0.127  #> 5 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Social Distancing  0.294  0.0386 #> 6 Pittsburgh     1 2020-06-01 Symptoms           0.0457 0.0770

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: two value columns passed yin yang arguments. Everything else – x/y mapping, faceting, titles – plain ggplot2. legend titles default column names supplied (Twitter Google ).  symbol stays round regardless panel’s aspect ratio, need coord_fixed(). shape sized square units, like radius grid::circleGrob().
code: ggplot(pitts_tg, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("Pittsburgh Google & Twitter Incidence Rate (%)")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: Thirty weeks across nine categories lot ink one panel. goal read individual symbols rather scan overall texture, subset data: fewer cells means taichi drawn larger.
code: pitts_small <- subset(pitts_tg, week <= 6)  ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("The first six weeks, drawn large")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: yin defaults grey (luminance) ramp yang red ramp, echoing “ink seal” look classic taichi. choice , useful rule thumb put source want read intensity yin (eye reads darkness quickly) source want read warmth yang.

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: fish gets scale. yang_colors yin_colors accept color vector (usually hex codes), yang_name / yin_name relabel legends. extra argument ... forwarded auto-built fill scales, can, example, set common limits two legends share range, pass na.value. two fish need different scale options – entirely different scale type – hand scale object constructor yin_scale / yang_scale used verbatim.
code: ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(     yin = Twitter,  yin_name = "Twitter (%)",     yin_colors = c("#deebf7", "#3182bd", "#08306b"),     yang = Google, yang_name = "Google (%)",     yang_colors = c("#fee6ce", "#e6550d", "#7f2704")   ) +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: ggplot2 leaves margin around discrete continuous scales, can make taichi grid look like floating. remove_padding() trims — since v0.3.0 detects axis’s scale type , can still spell "c" (continuous) / "d" (discrete) want override detection.
code: ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   remove_padding() +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: geom_taichi() ordinary layer, faceting works box. states_tg data set carries measurements across four states; pairing two weeks keeps every glyph large legible.
code: two_states <- subset(states_tg, state %in% c("New York", "Texas") & week <= 6)  ggplot(two_states, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   facet_wrap(~ state, ncol = 1) +   remove_padding(x = "c", y = "d") +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("New York vs Texas, weeks 1-6")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: theme_taichi() light, -white companion theme bottoms legends, drops panel grid ticks, emphasizes axis labels. normal ggplot2 theme, can override element afterwards, skip entirely bring .
code: ggplot(pitts_small, aes(x = week, y = category)) +   geom_taichi(yin = Twitter, yang = Google) +   theme_taichi() +   theme(plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white")) +   ggtitle("theme_taichi(), then tweaked")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: angle argument rotates glyph given number degrees. can constant (angle every cell) column name (one angle per cell), encoding directional temporal variable orientation.
code: one_rot <- data.frame(   x = c(1, 2, 1, 2),   y = c(2, 2, 1, 1),   yin = c(3, 5, 7, 9),   yang = c(9, 7, 5, 3),   rot = c(0, 45, 90, 180) )  ggplot(one_rot, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin, yang = yang, angle = rot,               limits = c(0, 10)) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: Setting eyes = TRUE draws classic taichi dots, sitting fish’s head: yin eye top bulb, yang eye bottom one. default white black dots glyph looks exactly like traditional symbol.  eyes just decoration: yin_eye_size, yang_eye_size, yin_eye_colour, yang_eye_colour accept either constant unquoted column name, two dots can encode two variables – fifth sixth dimension top x, y, two fills. mapped size column rescaled eye radii 5% 30% glyph radius (values already 0 0.5 used exact proportions, NA suppresses eye cell).
code: one_eye <- data.frame(   x = c(1, 2, 1, 2),   y = c(2, 2, 1, 1),   yin = c(3, 5, 7, 9),   yang = c(9, 7, 5, 3) )  ggplot(one_eye, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin, yang = yang, eyes = TRUE,               limits = c(0, 10)) +  # shared limits keep the palest fish visible   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi() one_eye$reach   <- c(10, 40, 25, 5)   # drives the yin eye one_eye$quality <- c(2, 1, 4, 8)      # drives the yang eye  ggplot(one_eye, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin, yang = yang,               eyes = TRUE,               yin_eye_size = reach,               yang_eye_size = quality,               limits = c(0, 10)) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: geom_taichi() now automatically detects whether yin / yang columns numeric discrete (factor / character / logical) picks appropriate scale – computed expressions factor(week) work . default palettes discrete colors sampled ramp skipping palest end, every category stays visible.  full control, hand fill scale – object constructor function – yin_scale / yang_scale; overrides auto-detection *_colors vectors entirely:
code: disc <- data.frame(   x = c(1, 2, 1, 2),   y = c(2, 2, 1, 1),   method = factor(c("A", "B", "C", "A")),   outcome = factor(c("win", "loss", "win", "loss")) )  ggplot(disc, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = method, yang = outcome) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi() ggplot(disc, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = method, yang = outcome,               yin_scale = scale_fill_viridis_d,               yang_scale = scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "outcome", option = "rocket",                                                 begin = 0.4, end = 0.8)) +   coord_fixed() +   theme_taichi()

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: fish whose fill value NA painted scale’s na.value colour (grey default; pass e.g. na.value = "transparent" ... hide ), one missing source never suppresses fish. na.rm = TRUE additionally drops rows missing positions, NA eye size simply skips cell’s eye.

title: Introduction to ggtaichi

what: Shared limits and a single legend
title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: two sources measured units, two separate legends noise. shared_limits = TRUE aligns limits fill scales (union range two columns, union levels two discrete sources), equal values carry equal ink. shared_legend = TRUE goes : fish use yin palette one legend shown. synthetic cafes_tg data natural demo — espresso matcha orders per 100 customers:  diverging data (values around 0), pass diverging palette color arguments symmetric limits ..., e.g. limits = c(-5, 5) — fish hinge midpoint.
code: ggplot(cafes_tg, aes(x = week, y = neighbourhood)) +   geom_taichi(yin = matcha, yang = espresso,               shared_legend = TRUE,               yin_name = "orders / 100 customers") +   remove_padding() +   theme_taichi() +   ggtitle("Espresso (yang) vs matcha (yin)")

title: Introduction to ggtaichi

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: cells layer now drawn one batched polygon (one batch eye dots) resolved draw time, instead one grob stack per cell. Large grids animation frames render several times faster, glyphs stay perfectly round resize device.

title: Introduction to ggtaichi
text: taichi grid best comparing two sources cell cell question — interlocking fish put numbers one glance. honest caveats: Dense grids become texture. Past roughly thousand cells stop reading symbols start reading fields; still useful spotting bands regime changes, precise lookup, subset (done ) facet. Luminance coarse channel. Small differences fish’s shade hard judge; exact comparison matters, add shared limits (shared_limits = TRUE) least two fish footing, consider printing numbers alongside. Color-vision deficiency. default grey ramp luminance-safe, default red ramp varies strongly luminance well. fully colorblind-safe plots, supply viridis scales: yin_scale = ggplot2::scale_fill_viridis_c (second option like "magma" yang) — every figure vignette can redrawn way one argument per fish. One source missing? NA fish keeps place (painted na.value), absence visible rather silently dropped.

what: BUG-1 — ... never reaches the layers; only the fill scales [P0]
title: ggtaichi — Next Release Brainstorm
text: geom_taichi(..., width = 0.8) → Error: unused argument (width = 0.8) geom_taichi(..., na.rm = TRUE) → unused argument geom_taichi(..., alpha = 0.5) → unused argument Consequences: users set cell width/height, alpha, na.rm, show.legend, suppress one two legends. Worse, ... applied fish scales, give yin yang scales different options (e.g. different limits/na.value) — limits= forced shared whether want . Fix direction: separate geom params scale params (e.g. dedicated yin_args/yang_args lists, explicit width/height/alpha/ show.legend arguments), document precisely ... targets.

what: BUG-2 — uses the deprecated size aesthetic instead of linewidth [P0]
title: ggtaichi — Next Release Brainstorm
text: geom’s outline width driven size (default_aes size = 0.1; draw_taichi() computes lwd = coords$size * .pt). Since ggplot2 3.4.0 outline-width aesthetic non-point geoms linewidth. Verified ggplot2 4.0.3: ... linewidth = 2 → Warning: Ignoring unknown parameters: linewidth, aes(linewidth = ...) → Ignoring unknown aesthetics: linewidth. legacy size honored, contrary every modern ggplot2 geom. Fix: rename linewidth default_aes draw_taichi() (keep size fallback/deprecation path back-compat). Tie §3e.

text: yin unquoted column name (string naming column) yin (dark) fish taichi symbol. yang unquoted column name (string naming column) yang (light) fish taichi symbol. yin_name label name (quotes) legend yin rendering. Default NULL (uses column name). yang_name label name (quotes) legend yang rendering. Default NULL (uses column name). yin_colors color vector, usually hex codes, yin fish fill. Used gradient continuous data discrete palette factor/character data. Ignored yin_scale provided. yang_colors color vector, usually hex codes, yang fish fill. Used gradient continuous data discrete palette factor/character data. Ignored yang_scale provided. yin_scale optional fill scale yin fish: either ready scale object scale constructor function (e.g. ggplot2::scale_fill_viridis_d). Overrides auto-detection. yang_scale optional fill scale yang fish, yin_scale. angle Rotation glyph degrees, counter-clockwise: either single number unquoted column name (one angle per cell). eyes Logical. TRUE, draws classic taichi eyes (dots), centred fish's head. Default FALSE, preserving plain v0.1.0 look. yin_eye_size, yang_eye_size Size eye proportion glyph radius: constant (default 0.15) unquoted data column encode variable (see Eyes section rescaling rule). yin_eye_colour, yang_eye_colour Colour eye dot: constant (defaults "white" "black") unquoted data column containing colour strings. shared_limits TRUE sources type (continuous, discrete), two auto-built fill scales share common limits — union range (union levels) yin yang — equal values read equal ink. title: Taichi — geom_taichi
code: library(ggplot2)  # taichi with numeric fills  data <- data.frame(x = rep(c(1, 2, 3), 3),                    y = rep(c(1, 2, 3), each = 3),                    yin_values = 1:9,                    yang_values = 9:1)  ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin_values,               yang = yang_values)   # categorical (discrete) fills are detected automatically  data$yin_class <- rep(c("low", "mid", "high"), 3)  ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin_class,               yang = yang_values)   # classic eyes, rotation, and data-driven eye sizes  ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +   geom_taichi(yin = yin_values,               yang = yang_values,               eyes = TRUE,               yin_eye_size = yang_values,               angle = 45)

title: ggtaichi 0.3.0
text: shared_limits = TRUE gives auto-built fill scales common limits — union range two sources (union levels discrete) — equal values read equal ink. Explicit limits passed ... still win, mixing discrete continuous source warns ignores flag. shared_legend = TRUE treats sources one measure: implies shared limits, paints fish yin_colors, drops duplicate yang guide, titles single legend “yin / yang” unless yin_name supplied. fish geoms exported (§4d): geom_yin_fish() geom_yang_fish() now documented exports (GeomYinFish / GeomYangFish ggproto objects available extension packages), users want single fish full manual control scale stacking. remove_padding() auto mode: called arguments now detects axis’s scale type plot added ; explicit "c" / "d" arguments remain overrides. New dataset cafes_tg: small, clearly synthetic (seeded) espresso vs. matcha dataset whose two columns share units — evergreen demo shared-scale features break COVID-era examples. generating script ships data-raw/. geom_taichi() now returns object friendly print() method instead dumping raw list internals console.

title: ggtaichi 0.3.0
text: Vectorized rendering: layer now draws cells one id-batched polygon (plus one batched circle grob eyes), resolved physical panel size draw time via makeContent(). Glyphs stay perfectly round resize, large grids render order magnitude faster: 1200-cell grid eyes takes 0.24 s build draw versus 3.5 s 0.2.0 (~15x, machine). drawn output pixel-identical 0.2.0 (verified rotated, eyed, outlined, faceted-discrete test plots).

title: ggtaichi 0.3.0
text: New pkgdown-gallery article showing palettes, data-driven eyes, rotation, categorical fills, shared scales, dense-grid texture. New “() use taichi” section intro vignette: honest guidance dense grids, luminance precision, colorblind-safe palettes (viridis via yin_scale / yang_scale), NA visibility.

title: ggtaichi 0.2.0
text: Data-driven eyes (eyes = TRUE): draw classic taichi dots, centred fish’s head (yin top bulb, yang bottom bulb). yin_eye_size / yang_eye_size yin_eye_colour / yang_eye_colour accept either constant unquoted data column, single glyph can now encode six dimensions (x, y, two fills, two eyes) (#3b). Rotation (angle): rotate glyph constant number degrees data column, encoding directional temporal variable orientation, unlocking spin animations (#3a). Categorical fill support: geom_taichi() inspects plot data + time auto-selects scale_fill_manual() discrete (factor / character / logical) yin / yang values — including computed expressions factor(week) — scale_fill_gradientn() continuous ones. default color vectors, discrete categories sample ramp evenly skipping palest end, category invisible white panel. Custom scales (objects constructors) can supplied via yin_scale / yang_scale (#4a, BUG-4). yin yang also accept strings naming column (yin = "Twitter"), previously produced meaningless constant fill. Animation vignette: vignette("animations") documents geom_taichi() composes gganimate (transition_states(), spin animations via angle, export recipes). gganimate Suggests-dependency.

title: ggtaichi 0.2.0
text: ... routing (BUG-1): geom parameters (alpha, colour, linewidth, linetype, width, height, na.rm, show.legend) now real, documented arguments geom_taichi() forwarded underlying fish layers. ... reserved options applied fill scales (e.g. shared limits); per-fish scale control goes yin_scale / yang_scale. linewidth aesthetic (BUG-2): outline width now uses modern linewidth aesthetic. Passing size geom_taichi() still works warns routed linewidth, inherited aes(size = ...) mapping renamed ggplot2’s built-deprecation path. ggtaichi now requires ggplot2 >= 3.4.0. Missing-argument validation (BUG-3): omitting yin yang errors immediately clear message instead silently producing degenerate grey plot, yin / yang column exist plot data errors + time offending name. Categorical fills (BUG-4): factor / character columns longer trigger cryptic “Discrete value supplied continuous scale” error (see categorical fill support ).

title: ggtaichi 0.2.0
text: Added testthat suite (argument validation, taichi_fish() geometry Monte-Carlo tiling check, parameter routing, rotation, eyes, discrete-scale selection, grob-level rendering checks) plus vdiffr visual-regression snapshots. geom_taichi() now returns ggtaichi_plot object added plot via ggplot_add() method, makes data-aware scale selection possible.
